en.extract:
The ground cuckoo-shrike (Coracina maxima) is an uncommon bird species endemic to Australia, occurring mainly in open woodland and arid grasslands throughout inland Australia, but also occasionally in areas on the east coast.


Taxonomy
Coracina maxima is one of 81 species in the family Campephagidae, 7 of which occur in Australia. The family can be divided into 2 groups, one of which contains 13 of the 81 species, and occur only in Asia. The other group, which includes genus Coracina (cuckoo-shrikes, cicadabirds and trillers), occurs in Africa, southern and Southeast Asia, Australia and islands in the west Pacific. Occurring in Australia are four species of cuckoo-shrike. The black-faced cuckoo-shrike (Coracina novaehollandiae) is a common species that occurs throughout all of Australia. The white-bellied cuckoo-shrike (Coracina papuensis) is uncommon and only occurring in the northern parts of the Northern Territory, as well as most of Queensland, Victoria and eastern New South Wales. The barred cuckoo-shrike (Coracina lineata) is a rare species and only occurs along the east coast of Queensland, and parts of the NSW coast. These three cuckoo-shrikes all occur in other countries including Indonesia, Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands. The ground cuckoo-shrike is an uncommon species and is endemic to Australia, occurring in parts of all the mainland Australian states and territories.  


Description
The ground cuckoo-shrike is a slender, long-legged bird, the largest of the cuckoo-shrikes measuring 33–37 cm in length, and weighing approximately 115 g. It was named cuckoo-shrike not because it is affiliated with either the cuckoo or the shrike, but because of the similar features that it has to both these birds. The stout, hooked shrike-like beak and plumage which is similar to that of the cuckoo are the reason for its name.
The adult bird possesses a pale grey head and upper body, with pale yellow eyes. Its lower back, rump and underparts are white and finely barred with black, contrasting with the black wings and the slightly forked black tail. This forked tail is a characteristic specific to the ground cuckoo-shrike. The immature ground cuckoo-shrike is similar in appearance to the adult, but has fine, broken black barrings on the throat and upper parts and dark eyes with a dark eye-line, instead of a black mask with pale yellow eyes. The flight call of this bird is a distinctive pee-ew, pee-ew or chill-chill….kee-lik, keelick. Due to the colouration of the ground cuckoo-shrike, when in flight they can look quite black-and-white and so may be mistaken for an Australian magpie.


Distribution and habitat
The distribution of the ground cuckoo-shrike is widespread across Australia, mainly occurring in Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, Western Australia, Northern Territory, Australian Capital Territory and in some parts of Victoria. They occur mostly inland of the Great Dividing Range in Australia's semi-arid regions, but also occur in areas on the east coast. They have been found to occur in areas of open woodland, arid shrub-land and open grasslands that are dominated by dead trees and species such as mulga, cypress-pine and mallee-spinifex. Ground cuckoo-shrikes are more likely to occur in these habitats if they are located near watercourses, floodplains, creeks and wetlands. Because of the location of the preferred habitats, they are more frequently found in inland areas rather than coastal regions. Various reports have recorded their presence in woodland areas located on river channels and floodplains, mulga shrubland and cleared woodland regrowth areas.
Despite their large range throughout Australia, they are an uncommon species, thinly distributed and probably nomadic, which can make it hard to predict their location and where they can be expected to be seen. The current population trend of the ground cuckoo-shrike is said to be decreasing, possibly due to declines in the Murray-Mallee region since the mid 1970s. Dolby and Clarke (2014) clearly lists and describes places where the ground cuckoo-shrike can be expected to be spotted.


Behaviour


Feeding
The ground cuckoo-shrike, as its name suggests, mainly feeds on the ground, being adapted to this kind of feeding with their long legs and the ability to run quickly along the ground. They spend a lot of their time foraging on bare open ground in small groups for their food, which consists of mainly on insects. Their diet includes adult and larval arthropods such as praying mantis, grasshoppers, locust, ants and spiders.


Breeding and nesting
Ground cuckoo-shrikes are generally encountered in small groups of three or more. This is possibly because the young stay in the family group until the next breeding season, sometimes helping to feed the new young. They make their nests on branches or forks of trees 3 to 15 m high, with bark, grass, stems and other material, or use the old nests of magpie-larks or white-winged chough.
The breeding season is from August to November, with the birds forming monogamous pairs and laying between two and five eggs in the nest, which is sometimes shared with other females, as more than one female are known to lay eggs in the same nest during the same breeding season. The eggs are a glossy olive colour with brown/red-brown markings on them.


Threats and conservation status
Though the population of the ground cuckoo-shrike is decreasing, its conservation status is classed as least concern. In 2013, it was listed as vulnerable in Victoria under the Advisory List of Threatened Vertebrate Fauna in Victoria 2013.
A possible threat to the ground cuckoo-shrike is the increase in woody vegetation density. This would benefit most woodland bird species, but in the case of the ground cuckoo-shrike which dwells in open woodland habitats, it could have a detrimental effect on its population.

The '''ground cuckoo-shrike''' ('''''Coracina maxima''''') is an uncommon bird species [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Australia]], occurring mainly in open woodland and arid grasslands throughout inland Australia,<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |title=Bird Australia |date=2009 |publisher=Dorling Kindersley |author=Andrew, David |author2=Burnie, David |others=BirdLife International |isbn=978-1-74033-699-4 |location=Camberwell, Vic. |oclc=299097574}}</ref> but also occasionally in areas on the east coast.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |title=The field guide to the birds of Australia |author=Pizzey, Graham |date=2007 |publisher=Harper Collins |isbn=978-0-207-19935-6 |location=Pymble, N.S.W. |oclc=166314858}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
''Coracina maxima'' is one of 81 species in the family [[Cuckooshrike|Campephagidae]], 7 of which occur in Australia.<ref name=":0" /> The family can be divided into 2 groups, one of which contains 13 of the 81 species, and occur only in Asia. The other group, which includes genus ''[[Coracina]]'' (cuckoo-shrikes, [[Common cicadabird|cicadabirds]] and [[triller]]s), occurs in Africa, southern and Southeast Asia, Australia and islands in the west Pacific.<ref name=":0" /> Occurring in Australia are four species of cuckoo-shrike. The [[Black-faced cuckooshrike|black-faced cuckoo-shrike]] (''Coracina novaehollandiae'') is a common species that occurs throughout all of Australia.<ref name=":1" /> The [[White-bellied cuckooshrike|white-bellied cuckoo-shrike]] (''Coracina papuensis'') is uncommon and only occurring in the northern parts of the Northern Territory, as well as most of Queensland, Victoria and eastern New South Wales.<ref name=":1" /> The [[Barred cuckooshrike|barred cuckoo-shrike]] (''Coracina lineata'') is a rare species and only occurs along the east coast of Queensland, and parts of the NSW coast.<ref name=":1" /> These three cuckoo-shrikes all occur in other countries including Indonesia, Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands.<ref name=":1" /> The ground cuckoo-shrike is an uncommon species and is endemic to Australia, occurring in parts of all the mainland Australian states and territories.<ref name=":0" />  

==Description==
The ground cuckoo-shrike is a slender, long-legged bird,<ref name=":1" /> the largest of the cuckoo-shrikes measuring 33–37 cm in length,<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|title=Birds of Australia. A Photographic Guide|last1=Campbell|first1=Iain|last2=Woods|first2=Sam|last3=Leseberg|first3=Nick|publisher=Princeton University Press|year=2014|isbn=978-1-4008-6510-9|location=Princeton|page=306}}</ref> and weighing approximately 115 g.<ref name=":0" /> It was named cuckoo-shrike not because it is affiliated with either the [[cuckoo]] or the [[shrike]], but because of the similar features that it has to both these birds.<ref name=":0" /> The stout, hooked shrike-like beak and plumage which is similar to that of the cuckoo are the reason for its name.<ref name=":0" />

The adult bird possesses a pale grey head and upper body, with pale yellow eyes.<ref name=":1" /> Its lower back, rump and underparts are white and finely barred with black, contrasting with the black wings and the slightly forked black tail.<ref name=":1" /> This forked tail is a characteristic specific to the ground cuckoo-shrike.<ref name=":2" /> The immature ground cuckoo-shrike is similar in appearance to the adult, but has fine, broken black barrings on the throat and upper parts and dark eyes with a dark eye-line, instead of a black mask with pale yellow eyes.<ref name=":1" /> The flight call of this bird is a distinctive ''pee-ew, pee-ew'' or ''chill-chill….kee-lik, keelick''.<ref name=":5">{{Cite book|title=Regional Field Guide to Birds. Red Centre to the Top End |last1=Pizzey |first1=Graham |last2=Knight |first2=Frank |publisher=Harper Collins Publishers |year=2013 |location=Sydney |page=91 |isbn=978-0-732-29537-0 |oclc=866840667}}</ref> Due to the colouration of the ground cuckoo-shrike, when in flight they can look quite black-and-white and so may be mistaken for an Australian magpie.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://www.graemechapman.com/library/viewphotos.php?c=95|title=Ground Cuckoo-shrike - Australian Birds - photographs by Graeme Chapman|website=www.graemechapman.com.au|access-date=2019-06-10}}{{Dead link|date=June 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The distribution of the ground cuckoo-shrike is widespread across Australia, mainly occurring in [[Queensland]], [[New South Wales]], [[South Australia]], [[Western Australia]], [[Northern Territory]], [[Australian Capital Territory]] and in some parts of Victoria.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|title=Finding Australian Birds: A Field Guide to Birding Locations|last1=Dolby|first1=Tim|last2=Clarke|first2=Rohan|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|year=2014|isbn=978-0-643-09766-7|location=Collingwood|pages=560}}</ref> They occur mostly inland of the Great Dividing Range<ref name=":1" /> in Australia's semi-arid regions,<ref name=":0" /> but also occur in areas on the east coast.<ref name=":1" /> They have been found to occur in areas of open woodland, arid shrub-land<ref name=":4" /> and open grasslands that are dominated by dead trees and species such as [[Acacia aneura|mulga]], cypress-pine and mallee-spinifex.<ref name=":1" /> Ground cuckoo-shrikes are more likely to occur in these habitats if they are located near watercourses, floodplains, creeks and wetlands.<ref name=":1" /> Because of the location of the preferred habitats, they are more frequently found in inland areas rather than coastal regions.<ref name=":3" /> Various reports have recorded their presence in woodland areas located on river channels and floodplains,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Pavey|first1=C. R.|last2=Nano|first2=C. E. M.|date=2009|title=Bird assemblages of arid Australia: Vegetation patterns have a greater effect than disturbance and resource pulses|journal=Journal of Arid Environments|volume=73|issue=6–7|page=638|doi=10.1016/j.jaridenv.2009.01.010 |bibcode=2009JArEn..73..634P }}</ref> mulga shrubland<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Burbidge|first1=A. A.|last2=Fuller|first2=P. J.|date=2007|title=Gibson desert birds: Responses to drought and plenty|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=107|issue=2|page=131|doi=10.1071/MU06044|bibcode=2007EmuAO.107..126B |s2cid=84571245}}</ref> and cleared woodland regrowth areas.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hannah|first1=D.|last2=Woinarsk|first2=J. C. Z.|last3=Catterall|first3=C. P.|author-link3=Carla P. Catterall|last4=McCosker|first4=J. C.|last5=Thurgate|first5=N. Y.|last6=Fensham|first6=R. J.|date=2007|title=Impacts of clearing, fragmentation and disturbance on the bird fauna of Eucalypt savanna woodlands in central Queensland, Australia|journal=Austral Ecology|volume=32|issue=3|page=267|doi=10.1111/j.1442-9993.2007.01683.x |bibcode=2007AusEc..32..261H }}</ref>

Despite their large range throughout Australia, they are an uncommon species, thinly distributed and probably nomadic, which can make it hard to predict their location and where they can be expected to be seen.<ref name=":2" /> The current population trend of the ground cuckoo-shrike is said to be decreasing, possibly due to declines in the Murray-Mallee region since the mid 1970s.<ref name=":7">{{Cite web|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|title=The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|access-date=2019-06-10}}</ref> Dolby and Clarke (2014) clearly lists and describes places where the ground cuckoo-shrike can be expected to be spotted.<ref name=":4" />

== Behaviour ==

=== Feeding ===
The ground cuckoo-shrike, as its name suggests, mainly feeds on the ground,<ref name=":5" /> being adapted to this kind of feeding with their long legs and the ability to run quickly along the ground.<ref name=":6">{{Cite book|title=Mallee bird communities and the pastoral industry in semi-arid South Australia|last=Williams|first=S. L.|year=1994|page=131}}</ref> They spend a lot of their time foraging on bare open ground in small groups<ref name=":2" /> for their food, which consists of mainly on insects.<ref name=":6" /> Their diet includes adult and larval [[arthropod]]s such as [[Mantis|praying mantis]], [[grasshopper]]s, [[locust]], [[ant]]s and [[spider]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|title=The food of Australian Birds 2: Passerines|last1=Barker|first1=R. D.|last2=Vestjens|first2=W. J. M.|publisher=Victoria: CSIRO Publishing|year=1990|isbn=978-0-643-05115-7|location=Melbourne|pages=31}}</ref>

=== Breeding and nesting ===
Ground cuckoo-shrikes are generally encountered in small groups of three or more.<ref name=":6" /> This is possibly because the young stay in the family group until the next breeding season, sometimes helping to feed the new young.<ref name=":3" /> They make their nests on branches or forks of trees 3 to 15 m high, with bark, grass, stems and other material,<ref name=":1" /> or use the old nests of [[magpie-lark]]s or [[white-winged chough]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Birds of the Barham district, New South Wales and Victoria: an historical summary 1930-1999|last=Disher|first=Peter|publisher=Barham Land Care Group in association with Bird Observers Club of Australia|year=2000|isbn=0-646-40393-1|location=Barham, N.S.W.|pages=47}}</ref>

The [[breeding season]] is from August to November,<ref name=":1" /> with the birds forming monogamous pairs and laying between two and five eggs in the nest, which is sometimes shared with other females, as more than one female are known to lay eggs in the same nest during the same breeding season.<ref name=":0" /> The eggs are a glossy olive colour with brown/red-brown markings on them.<ref name=":1" />

== Threats and conservation status ==
Though the population of the ground cuckoo-shrike is decreasing, its conservation status is classed as least concern.<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" /> In 2013, it was listed as vulnerable in Victoria under the Advisory List of Threatened Vertebrate Fauna in Victoria 2013.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://bie.ala.org.au/species/urn:lsid:biodiversity.org.au:afd.taxon:8538fad3-cf91-4804-8248-d6a921d0e7c4|title=Coracina (Pteropodocys) maxima: Ground Cuckoo-Shrike|last=Australia|first=Atlas of Living|website=bie.ala.org.au|language=en-AU|access-date=2019-06-10}}</ref>

A possible threat to the ground cuckoo-shrike is the increase in woody vegetation density. This would benefit most woodland bird species, but in the case of the ground cuckoo-shrike which dwells in open woodland habitats, it could have a detrimental effect on its population.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kutt|first1=A. S.|last2=Martin|first2=T. G.|date=2010|title=Bird foraging height predicts bird species response to woody vegetation change|journal=Biodiversity and Conservation|volume=19|issue=8|page=2257|doi=10.1007/s10531-010-9840-y|bibcode=2010BiCon..19.2247K |s2cid=34308592}}</ref>

zh.extract:
细嘴地鹃鵙（学名：Coracina maxima）是山椒鸟科鸦鹃鵙属的一种，为澳大利亚的特有种。其种加词“maxima”意为“大的”。


保护现状
澳大利亚《环境保护和生物多样性保护法》并未将细嘴地鹃鵙列入受威胁名录中。


参考文献
BirdLife International. Coracina maxima. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2009.   [2010-10-22] （英语）.
